title: What is a method
instruction: Think of a method as a behaviour that belongs to a specific type. A `Book` can `Describe()`. A `Member` can `Greet()`.\nIn Go, you attach a function to a type by adding a *receiver* between `func` and the name.\nRun this. The `Greet` method on `Member` prints a greeting with the member's name.
successMessage: `m.Greet()` — the Member greets themselves. Clean, readable, and the method knows exactly who it belongs to.

title: Define a method
instruction: Write your own method. Add a `Describe()` method to `Book` that prints: `"The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald (1925)"`\nFormat: `Title by Author (Year)`. Then call it.
hint: func (b Book) Describe() { fmt.Println(b.Title, "by", b.Author, "(" + strconv.Itoa(b.Year) + ")") }
expectedOutput: The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald (1925)

codeChecks.message: Define a Describe() method on Book: func (b Book) Describe() { ... }
Call b.Describe() in main.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your method, your type, your call. The Book now knows how to describe itself.

title: ahha — value receiver cannot change the original
instruction: A value receiver receives a copy. Changes inside the method don't affect the original.\nRun this. Notice that calling `tryRename` does not change `b.Title` — because it got a copy.
successMessage: Brave New World inside, 1984 outside. The method changed its copy, not the original. Value receivers are safe for read-only operations.

title: Pointer receiver — actually changes the struct
instruction: To modify the original struct from a method, use a pointer receiver: `(b *Book)`.\nNow the method receives the address — not a copy — and changes stick.\nAdd a `CheckOut()` method with a pointer receiver that sets `b.IsCheckedOut` to `true`. Call it and print the status before and after.
hint: func (b *Book) CheckOut() { b.IsCheckedOut = true }  then b.CheckOut()

codeChecks.message: Define CheckOut() with a pointer receiver: func (b *Book) CheckOut() { b.IsCheckedOut = true }
Call b.CheckOut() and print IsCheckedOut after.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: false before, true after. The pointer receiver method reached into the original struct and changed it.

title: Value vs pointer receiver — side by side
instruction: Rule of thumb: reading only? Use a value receiver. Mutating the struct? Use a pointer receiver. Large struct? Use a pointer receiver to avoid copying.\nRun this. `Describe()` reads, `CheckOut()` mutates — both on `Book`.
successMessage: Describe uses a value receiver — it only reads. CheckOut uses a pointer receiver — it mutates. Mix both on the same type freely.

title: Methods with parameters and return values
instruction: Methods accept parameters and return values just like regular functions.\nAdd an `IsAvailable() bool` method that returns `true` if `IsCheckedOut == false`, and a `DaysOverdue(daysBorrowed int) int` method that returns `daysBorrowed - 14` if positive, else 0. Call both and print the results.

codeChecks.message: Add IsAvailable() bool method to Book.
Add DaysOverdue(daysBorrowed int) int method to Book.
Replace the TODO comments with your methods.

successMessage: Moby-Dick is not available and is 6 days overdue. The Book type now knows how to answer real library questions.

title: Methods on non-struct types
instruction: You can add methods to any named type — not just structs.\n`type ISBN string` is a named type based on `string`. You can attach methods to it.\nAdd an `IsValid() bool` method to `ISBN` that returns `true` if the length is 13. Call it and print the result.
hint: func (i ISBN) IsValid() bool { return len(i) == 13 }

codeChecks.message: Add an IsValid() bool method to ISBN.
Check if len(i) == 13 to validate ISBN.
Call isbn.IsValid() and print the result.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Methods on a string-based type. You just made ISBN numbers smarter. This pattern is used for units, currencies, IDs.

title: String() — fmt prints it automatically
instruction: If you add a `String() string` method to your type, `fmt.Println` calls it automatically.\nThis is Go's way of letting your types control how they are printed.\nAdd `String() string` to `Member` that returns `"Alice (Card: 1001)"`. Then just call `fmt.Println(m)` and see it use your method.
hint: func (m Member) String() string { return fmt.Sprintf("%s (Card: %d)", m.Name, m.CardNumber) }

codeChecks.message: Add String() string method to Member.
Use fmt.Sprintf to format the string inside String().
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: fmt.Println called your String() method without you asking it to. This is Go's interface system at work — and you just used it without knowing.

title: Fix the broken method
instruction: The `ReturnBook` method below is supposed to mark a book as returned and clear who checked it out. But it does not work — the book stays checked out after the call.\nFind the bug and fix it.
hint: The method uses a value receiver (b Book). Change it to a pointer receiver (b *Book) so it modifies the original.

codeChecks.message: Change the receiver to *Book: func (b *Book) ReturnBook()

successMessage: One character change — (b Book) to (b *Book) — and it works. The most common method bug in Go.

title: Build a library system
instruction:
No starter code. Build two types with methods:
**Book** (Title, Author, IsCheckedOut bool) — CheckOut(), Return(), Describe()
**LibraryMember** (Name, BooksBorrowed int) — BorrowBook(), ReturnBook(), Describe()
Create one of each, perform some transactions, and call Describe on both.
hint: Use pointer receivers for CheckOut/Return since they mutate; value receiver for Describe.
expectedOutput: The Hobbit by J.R.R. Tolkien | Available: true
Alice has borrowed 3 books this month

codeChecks.message: Define a Book struct.
Define a LibraryMember struct.
Add a CheckOut() method to Book.
Add a Return() method to Book.
Add a Describe() method to Book.
Add a BorrowBook() method to LibraryMember.
Add a ReturnBook() method to LibraryMember.
Add a Describe() method to LibraryMember.

successMessage: Two types. Six methods. One clean program. You just built a fully object-oriented library system in Go — without a single class keyword.
